message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 25 (16 todo)
Recent changes: Added claim(policy, incident) with INSURANCE_VALUES table; payout = sum of (amount − 100 deductible) per damage; cap = 2× sum of insurance values; remainingCap = cap − payout.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Note: upcoming claim tests add per-item special clauses (high-enchantment >=8 → 50% of damage; dragon material → full), per-event deductible nuances, and cap exhaustion across successive claims.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 25 tests pass. Now I'll begin the Refactor phase analysis.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added the `claim` function and its helpers/constants. Let me evaluate naming of what was just introduced:

- **`claim`** — clearly reveals intent: processes an insurance claim. Keep.
- **`ClaimPolicy`, `ClaimDamage`, `ClaimIncident`, `ClaimResult`** — descriptive, domain-aligned. Keep.
- **`DEDUCTIBLE`, `CAP_MULTIPLIER`, `INSURANCE_VALUES`** — clear. Keep.
- **Inside `claim`**: local `payout`, `insuranceTotal`, `cap` are descriptive. Keep.

Naming is good. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (of the new `claim` function)

```
export function claim(policy, incident): ClaimResult {
  let payout = 0;                                   // binding(payout)=1, constant(0)=1, assignment=6
  for (const damage of incident.damages) {          // loop=5, binding(damage)=1, invocation(.damages)=2
    payout += damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE;           // assignment=6, invocation(.amount)=2, binding(DEDUCTIBLE)=1, invocation(-)=2
  }
  let insuranceTotal = 0;                            // binding=1, constant=1, assignment=6
  for (const item of policy.items) {                 // loop=5, binding(item)=1, invocation(.items)=2
    insuranceTotal += INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type];  // assignment=6, invocation(index)=2, binding(INSURANCE_VALUES)=1, invocation(.type)=2
  }
  const cap = CAP_MULTIPLIER * insuranceTotal;       // binding(cap)=1, binding(CAP_MULTIPLIER)=1, invocation(*)=2, binding(insuranceTotal)=1
  return { payout, remainingCap: cap - payout };     // binding x3 + invocation(-)=2
}
```

Total mass ≈ **72** for the `claim` function body.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** ✅ All 25 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The two `for` loops with manual `let ... = 0` accumulators are the lower-level "how". The rest of the file already established a clear idiom: small named helper functions (`policyBasePremiumFor`, `itemSurchargesFor`, etc.) that the orchestrating function composes. The new `claim` inlines two summation loops, which is **inconsistent** with the established style and mixes "what" (payout, cap) with "how" (loop accumulation). Extracting two helpers — `payoutFor(incident)` and `capFor(policy)` — would make `claim` read as a clear composition and match the file's existing patterns.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** Both loops are accumulation loops. There's a structural similarity but they sum different things over different collections — not true knowledge duplication. The upcoming tests (per-item clauses, per-item insurance) will make these diverge further, so forcing a shared abstraction now would be premature (violates Rule 4). I'll leave that alone.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Extraction adds two function elements but removes complexity from the orchestrator. Given the established file convention and the clarity gain, this is justified.

The best improvement: extract `payoutFor` and `capFor` to match the existing composition style and reveal intent. This is also forward-friendly — the noted upcoming clause/cap logic will naturally live inside these focused helpers.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

content.text:
All 25 tests still pass.

## Step 5 (cont.): New APP Mass

The orchestrating `claim` function dropped from ~72 to:

```
export function claim(policy, incident): ClaimResult {
  const payout = payoutFor(incident);          // binding(payout)=1, invocation=2, binding(incident)=1
  const cap = capFor(policy);                  // binding(cap)=1, invocation=2, binding(policy)=1
  return { payout, remainingCap: cap - payout }; // binding x3, invocation(-)=2
}
```

`claim` body ≈ **16** (down from ~72). The two loops moved into focused helpers, lowering the orchestrator's mass and concentrating the loop complexity where it belongs. Total file mass is roughly unchanged (the loops were relocated, not removed) but is now better distributed — Rule 2 (intent) improved without sacrificing correctness.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ Naming: Evaluated all new identifiers (`claim`, `ClaimPolicy/Damage/Incident/Result`, `DEDUCTIBLE`, `CAP_MULTIPLIER`, `INSURANCE_VALUES`). All clearly reveal intent — no renames needed.
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `payoutFor(incident)` and `capFor(policy)`. The `claim` function now reads as a declarative composition ("payout, then cap, then remaining"), matching the file's established helper-composition idiom used throughout `quote` (e.g. `policyBasePremiumFor`, `itemSurchargesFor`).
- ✅ Mass: Orchestrator `claim` mass reduced ~72 → ~16; loop complexity relocated into named, single-responsibility helpers.

**Deliberately NOT done:**
- ❌ Did not merge the two accumulation loops into a shared "sum" abstraction. They sum different things over different collections (knowledge is not duplicated), and the noted upcoming work — per-item high-enchantment/dragon-material clauses, per-event deductible nuances, and cap exhaustion across successive claims — will make `payoutFor` and `capFor` diverge further. Forcing premature abstraction now would violate Rule 4 (fewest elements / no over-engineering) and harm the upcoming changes.

The extraction is also forward-friendly: the upcoming per-item clause logic has a natural home inside `payoutFor`, and cap-exhaustion state fits around `capFor`.

## Step 7: Completion Report

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `payoutFor` and `capFor` from `claim` to reveal intent and match the file's existing composition style.
- **Mass Change:** `claim` orchestrator ~72 → ~16 (loop complexity relocated into focused helpers).
- **Tests:** All 25 passing ✅ (16 todo remain).

Files:
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_06-12-03_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_06-12-03_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

Proceeding to the next test: `"should pay out 100 for a rune damaged 200 (no special clause; damage minus deductible)"` (Red phase).
